facts.text: Copacabana Beach in Rio de Janeiro stretches for 2.5 miles along the Atlantic coast and is one of the most famous beaches in the world.
Sand is primarily made up of tiny fragments of rocks, minerals, and shells that have been broken down by weathering and erosion over thousands of years.
Waikiki Beach in Honolulu was once a retreat for Hawaiian royalty and its name means 'spouting waters' in Hawaiian.
The Atlantic City Boardwalk, built in 1870, was the first boardwalk in the United States and stretches over four miles along the New Jersey shore.
Sea turtles return to the same beach where they were born to lay their eggs, navigating across entire oceans using the Earth's magnetic field.
Grace Bay Beach in Turks and Caicos is consistently ranked among the best beaches in the world for its pristine turquoise water and powdery white sand.
Rip currents are responsible for about 80% of beach rescues performed by lifeguards in the United States each year.
The Beach Boys formed in Hawthorne, California in 1961 and became synonymous with the California surf and beach culture of the 1960s.
Glass Beach in Fort Bragg, California features colorful sea glass pebbles created from decades of dumped glass that was tumbled smooth by ocean waves.
The Maldives is the lowest-lying country on Earth, with an average ground level of just 1.5 meters above sea level, making its beaches especially vulnerable to rising seas.
Bondi Beach in Sydney, Australia hosted one of the world's first lifesaving clubs, the Bondi Surf Bathers' Life Saving Club, founded in 1907.
Pink sand beaches, like those in Harbour Island in the Bahamas, get their color from tiny red organisms called foraminifera that live on coral reefs.
The Santa Monica Pier, built in 1909, is a landmark on the California coast and marks the western terminus of historic Route 66.
Beach volleyball became an official Olympic sport at the 1996 Atlanta Games.
Papakolea Beach on Hawaii's Big Island is one of only four green sand beaches in the world, colored by the mineral olivine from a nearby volcanic cone.
The Gulf Coast of Florida is known for its calm, warm waters and white quartz sand beaches, particularly along the stretch from Clearwater to Siesta Key.
Dolphins are frequently spotted near beaches worldwide, often surfing waves for fun and hunting fish in shallow waters close to shore.
Navagio Beach (Shipwreck Beach) on the Greek island of Zakynthos is accessible only by boat and features the rusted hull of a smuggler's ship that ran aground in 1980.
Sandcastle competitions date back to the early 1900s, and modern competitive sandcastle builders use only sand and water with no adhesives.
UV rays can penetrate up to three feet of water, meaning you can still get sunburned while swimming or snorkeling.
Reynisfjara Beach in Iceland features dramatic black volcanic sand and towering basalt sea stacks, making it one of the most striking beaches on Earth.
Coney Island in Brooklyn, New York opened its first amusement park in 1895 and became one of America's most iconic beach destinations.
The world's longest beach is Praia do Cassino in Brazil, stretching approximately 157 miles along the southern coast.
Tide pools are formed when seawater gets trapped in rocky depressions at low tide, creating miniature ecosystems full of starfish, anemones, and small crabs.
Aruba's Eagle Beach is known for its wide stretch of white sand and iconic fofoti trees that lean dramatically due to constant trade winds.
The first professional lifeguards in the United States were employed at Atlantic City, New Jersey in 1892.
Maya Bay in Thailand, made famous by the 2000 movie 'The Beach' starring Leonardo DiCaprio, was closed for several years to allow its coral reefs to recover from overtourism.
Bioluminescent beaches, like those in Vaadhoo Island in the Maldives, glow at night due to phytoplankton that emit light when disturbed by waves.
South Beach in Miami is famous for its Art Deco Historic District, which features over 800 pastel-colored buildings from the 1930s and 1940s.
California's coastline stretches approximately 840 miles from the Oregon border to Mexico, featuring hundreds of public beaches.
Hermit crabs found on tropical beaches switch shells as they grow, sometimes forming 'vacancy chains' where multiple crabs trade shells in sequence.
World Oceans Day is celebrated annually to raise awareness about the importance of the ocean and its beaches.
The Seychelles archipelago has 115 islands with beaches featuring massive granite boulders, giving them a distinctive otherworldly appearance.
Venice Beach in Los Angeles is known for its bohemian boardwalk, street performers, Muscle Beach outdoor gym, and skate park.
Beach sand can reach temperatures of over 150°F (65°C) on hot summer days, hot enough to cause burns on bare feet.
Surfing originated in ancient Polynesia, with the earliest known references dating back to the 15th century in Hawaii.
The Caribbean island of Barbados is known as the birthplace of rum, and its beaches on the west coast are called the 'Platinum Coast.'
Whitehaven Beach in Australia's Whitsunday Islands has sand that is 98% pure silica, making it one of the whitest and most pristine beaches in the world.
Beach erosion in the United States costs an estimated $500 million per year in property damage and beach nourishment projects.
The television show Baywatch, which ran from 1989 to 2001, became one of the most-watched TV shows in history, airing in over 140 countries.
Fiji is made up of over 330 islands, about one-third of which are inhabited, with some of the most remote and untouched beaches in the Pacific.
National Seashell Day celebrates the hobby of beachcombing and shell collecting.
St. Barts in the Caribbean is known for its glamorous beach scene, particularly Shell Beach in Gustavia, which is covered in millions of tiny shells.
Piping plovers, small shorebirds that nest on Atlantic Coast beaches, are federally protected and beach sections are often closed during nesting season.
The Great Barrier Reef, visible from many Australian beaches, is the largest living structure on Earth and can be seen from space.
Myrtle Beach, South Carolina attracts about 20 million visitors annually, making it one of the most popular beach destinations on the US East Coast.
Black sand beaches are formed from volcanic minerals like basite and are found in places like Hawaii, Iceland, and the Canary Islands.
The sport of parasailing was invented in the 1960s and is now a popular activity at beach resorts around the world.
Ipanema Beach in Rio de Janeiro was immortalized by the 1962 bossa nova song 'The Girl from Ipanema,' one of the most recorded songs in history.
The US Virgin Islands, including St. Thomas, St. John, and St. Croix, feature over 60 beaches with crystal-clear Caribbean waters.
Waves are primarily generated by wind blowing across the surface of the ocean, and they can travel thousands of miles before reaching a beach.
International Surfing Day was created to celebrate the sport and raise awareness about ocean conservation.
Siesta Key Beach in Florida has been named America's best beach multiple times, thanks to its cool, powdery quartz sand that doesn't get hot underfoot.
The Malibu coast in California is home to numerous celebrity homes and world-class surf breaks like Surfrider Beach and Zuma Beach.
Zanzibar, an island off the coast of Tanzania, is famous for its spice trade history and stunning beaches with turquoise Indian Ocean waters.
Beachcombing, the activity of searching shorelines for interesting items washed ashore, has been a popular pastime for centuries.
Jellyfish stings affect an estimated 150 million people worldwide each year, with most stings occurring at beaches in tropical waters.
Virginia Beach, Virginia has a three-mile-long boardwalk and is one of the longest pleasure beaches in the world.
Red sand beaches, like Kokkini Beach in Santorini, Greece, get their color from iron-rich volcanic rock and minerals.
The modern bikini was introduced by French designer Louis Réard on July 5, 1946, and was named after Bikini Atoll.
Bora Bora in French Polynesia is surrounded by a lagoon and barrier reef, with overwater bungalows that have become iconic symbols of tropical luxury.
Ocean City, Maryland's boardwalk stretches three miles and is one of the most popular beach boardwalks on the US East Coast.
Australia has over 10,000 beaches, so many that if you visited a new one every day, it would take you over 27 years to see them all.
Beach flags use a color-coded system: green means safe, yellow means moderate hazard, red means high hazard, and double red means the water is closed to swimming.
The Dead Sea, bordered by Jordan and Israel, has beaches at the lowest elevation on Earth, about 1,410 feet below sea level.
Snorkeling became widely popular in the 1940s after the development of modern diving masks and snorkel tubes made from rubber and glass.
The Bahamas has over 700 islands and cays, featuring some of the clearest water in the world with visibility exceeding 200 feet.
Spring break beach culture in the United States became a widespread phenomenon in the 1960s, popularized by movies like 'Where the Boys Are.'
Mauritius, an island nation in the Indian Ocean, is home to an underwater waterfall illusion visible from the air that is actually sand and silt flowing off the coastal shelf.
The world's widest beach is at low tide in Mont Saint-Michel, France, where the sea can retreat as far as 15 kilometers from the shore.
National Sandcastle Day is celebrated to honor the art and creativity of building sandcastles.
Horseshoe crabs, which have been around for over 450 million years, come ashore on Delaware Bay beaches every spring to lay their eggs.
The Greek islands have over 200 inhabited islands, each with unique beaches ranging from black volcanic sand to white marble pebbles.
Hawaii's beaches come in nearly every color including white, black, red, and green, depending on the local volcanic and mineral geology.
The concept of the beach vacation became popular in the 18th century when doctors began prescribing sea air and bathing for health benefits.
Clearwater Beach in Florida is a consistent winner of TripAdvisor's best beach awards, known for its calm, shallow Gulf waters.
Tides are caused primarily by the gravitational pull of the moon and sun on Earth's oceans, creating predictable high and low water levels.
The Outer Banks of North Carolina is a 200-mile chain of barrier islands known for their wild beaches, shipwreck history, and the Wright Brothers' first flight.
Beach nourishment is the process of adding sand to eroding beaches, and the US Army Corps of Engineers has been doing it since the 1920s.
Turks and Caicos has more than 230 miles of pristine beaches across its 40 islands and cays.
International Coastal Cleanup Day brings together volunteers to clean beaches worldwide.
The surf break at Pipeline on Oahu's North Shore is considered one of the most dangerous and prestigious waves in professional surfing.
The world's most visited beach is Haeundae Beach in Busan, South Korea, which attracts over 10 million visitors annually.
Sand dunes along beaches are formed by wind carrying sand particles inland, and they serve as natural barriers against storm surges and flooding.
The Florida Keys stretch 120 miles from Key Largo to Key West, with beaches, coral reefs, and the only living barrier reef in North America.
Sandpipers, small shorebirds that run along the water's edge, can be found on beaches on every continent except Antarctica.
Santorini's beaches in Greece come in multiple colors including red, black, and white, each formed by different volcanic minerals.
The first sunscreen was developed by chemist Franz Greiter in 1946, inspired by getting sunburned while climbing Piz Buin in the Austrian Alps.
Cannon Beach in Oregon is known for Haystack Rock, a 235-foot sea stack that is one of the most recognizable landmarks on the Pacific Coast.
The world's hottest beach sand temperature was recorded at Lut Desert, Iran, where surface temperatures can exceed 159°F (70°C).
National Beach Day is celebrated annually to appreciate and protect the world's beaches.
Trunk Bay in St. John, US Virgin Islands features an underwater snorkeling trail with signs identifying coral and marine life.
Beach grass, also known as marram grass, plays a crucial role in stabilizing sand dunes and preventing coastal erosion.
The phenomenon of singing sand, where sand dunes produce a humming or booming sound, occurs at certain beaches around the world.
Hilton Head Island, South Carolina is known for its 12 miles of pristine beaches and is one of the top resort destinations in the US.
Surfing legend Duke Kahanamoku helped popularize the sport internationally in the early 20th century and is considered the father of modern surfing.
The Amalfi Coast in Italy features dramatic cliffside beaches and colorful villages perched above the Tyrrhenian Sea.
Loggerhead sea turtles nest on beaches along the southeastern US coast, with peak nesting season from May through August.
Tulum Beach in Mexico combines ancient Mayan ruins overlooking the Caribbean Sea with white sand beaches, creating one of the most photographed beach settings.
A single grain of sand is typically between 0.1 and 2 millimeters in diameter, and there are roughly 8,000 grains in a single teaspoon of sand.
Cape Cod in Massachusetts has over 559 miles of coastline with beaches facing both the Atlantic Ocean and Cape Cod Bay.
The Hamptons on Long Island, New York have been a summer retreat for New York City's wealthy since the 1800s, with beaches stretching along the South Fork.
Phuket, Thailand's largest island, features over 30 beaches ranging from bustling Patong to secluded Freedom Beach.
National Flip Flop Day celebrates the most iconic beach footwear.
The phenomenon of beach cusps, regularly spaced scalloped patterns in the sand along the shoreline, is still not fully understood by scientists.
Monterey Bay in California is home to one of the world's most diverse marine ecosystems, with tide pools teeming with life along its rocky beaches.
Shell Beach in Shark Bay, Western Australia is made up entirely of billions of tiny cockle shells stacked up to 10 meters deep.
The Riviera Maya in Mexico stretches 100 miles along the Caribbean coast, featuring white sand beaches, cenotes, and coral reefs.
Beach-launched fishing, where anglers cast directly from the shore, is popular on beaches from Cape Hatteras to Daytona Beach.
Anse Source d'Argent in the Seychelles is often called the most photographed beach in the world, with its dramatic granite boulders and shallow turquoise water.
The SPF in sunscreen stands for Sun Protection Factor, and SPF 30 blocks about 97% of UVB rays.
Point Reyes National Seashore in California has over 80 miles of coastline with dramatic cliffs, beaches, and elephant seal colonies.
The first World Surfing Championship was held at Manly Beach in Sydney, Australia in 1964.
Long Beach Island, New Jersey is an 18-mile-long barrier island with beaches, a historic lighthouse, and a classic boardwalk.
Horseshoe Bay Beach in Bermuda is famous for its pink sand, created by the shells of tiny red organisms called foraminifera mixed with white sand.
The Skeleton Coast in Namibia is one of the most remote and desolate beaches on Earth, littered with shipwrecks and whale bones.
Beach volleyball was invented in Santa Monica, California in the 1920s and has since become one of the world's most popular beach sports.
The annual migration of red crabs on Christmas Island sees millions of crabs crossing beaches to reach the ocean for spawning.
Pensacola Beach in Florida has sand made of pure quartz crystal that stays cool to the touch and squeaks when you walk on it.
The Côte d'Azur, or French Riviera, along the Mediterranean coast includes famous beach towns like Nice, Cannes, and Saint-Tropez.
Padre Island National Seashore in Texas is the longest undeveloped barrier island in the world, stretching 70 miles along the Gulf Coast.
Portuguese man-of-war, often found washed up on beaches, are not actually jellyfish but colonial organisms made up of specialized individual animals.
The annual sand sculpting world championship draws artists from around the globe who create elaborate sculptures using only sand and water.
World Sea Turtle Day raises awareness about the importance of protecting sea turtle nesting beaches.
Cape May, New Jersey is one of the oldest seaside resorts in the United States, with Victorian-era beach houses dating to the 1850s.
The phenomenon of rogue waves, unusually large and unexpected waves, can occur at any beach and are particularly dangerous for surfers and swimmers.
Ko Phi Phi in Thailand features stunning beaches surrounded by limestone cliffs and is a major destination for rock climbing and diving.
Assateague Island, straddling Maryland and Virginia, is famous for its wild horses that roam freely on the beach.
Seven Mile Beach in Grand Cayman is actually 5.5 miles long and is consistently ranked among the Caribbean's best beaches.
The first beach resort hotel in America was the Congress Hall in Cape May, New Jersey, built in 1816.
Ancient Romans had seaside resorts at Baiae near Naples, where wealthy citizens built elaborate villas along the beach.
Cottesloe Beach in Perth, Australia hosts an annual 'Sculptures by the Sea' exhibition that combines art with the natural beach setting.
The blue flag certification is an international award given to beaches that meet strict standards for water quality, environmental management, and safety.
National Learn to Swim Day promotes water safety education at beaches and pools.
Kailua Beach on Oahu, Hawaii has been named America's best beach by Dr. Beach and is known for its fine, powdery sand and gentle waves.
The Algarve region in southern Portugal features dramatic sea caves, golden cliffs, and over 100 beaches along its 100-mile coastline.
Green sea turtles can hold their breath for up to five hours and are frequently seen near beaches with seagrass beds.
Ocean Beach in San Francisco stretches 3.5 miles and is known for its strong currents, foggy conditions, and spectacular sunsets.
The world's most remote beach is thought to be on Bouvet Island in the South Atlantic, an uninhabited Norwegian territory surrounded by glaciers.
Cabo San Lucas at the tip of Mexico's Baja Peninsula is famous for its distinctive Land's End rock arch and Lover's Beach.
Sand fleas, also called mole crabs, live burrowed in the sand at the waterline and are an important food source for shorebirds.
Martha's Vineyard in Massachusetts has beaches ranging from the calm waters of Vineyard Haven to the dramatic cliffs of Gay Head (Aquinnah).
The color of ocean water at beaches depends on how sunlight interacts with the water, sand, and marine organisms, explaining why tropical waters appear more turquoise.
International Whale Shark Day celebrates the world's largest fish, which is often seen near tropical beaches.
Daytona Beach, Florida is famous for allowing cars to drive on the sand, a tradition dating back to its use as a car racing venue in the early 1900s.
The Skeleton Coast in Namibia gets its name from the whale and seal bones that once littered its beaches from the whaling industry.
Playa del Carmen in Mexico has transformed from a quiet fishing village to one of the most popular beach destinations in the Riviera Maya.
Beach plum bushes grow along the northeastern US coastline and produce small fruits that are used to make jams and jellies.
The annual Polar Bear Plunge at Coney Island, New York on New Year's Day has been a tradition since 1903.
Corals are living animals, not plants, and tropical beach ecosystems depend heavily on the health of nearby coral reefs.
North Shore of Oahu transforms from calm swimming beaches in summer to some of the world's biggest surfing waves in winter.
The Mediterranean Sea touches the coastlines of 22 countries, providing thousands of beaches across three continents.
Jekyll Island, Georgia is a barrier island with 10 miles of beaches, historic millionaire's cottages, and one of the state's most scenic coastlines.
Cocoa Beach, Florida is located near Kennedy Space Center and is known as a prime viewing spot for rocket launches from the beach.
Sand mining from beaches is a significant environmental problem worldwide, with an estimated 50 billion tons of sand and gravel extracted annually.
The Pacific Coast Highway in California runs along some of the most spectacular beach scenery in the world, from Big Sur to Malibu.
World Ocean Day was officially recognized by the United Nations in 2008.
Sanibel Island in Florida is one of the best shelling beaches in North America, with over 250 species of shells washing ashore.
The tallest sandcastle ever built stood over 69 feet tall and was constructed in Binz, Germany in 2019.
Zanzibar's Prison Island, just off the main island, features beaches where giant Aldabra tortoises roam freely.
Beach wrack, the piles of seaweed and debris that wash ashore, plays an important ecological role by providing nutrients and habitat for beach organisms.
Ninety Mile Beach in Victoria, Australia is actually about 90 miles long and is one of the few beaches whose name matches its real length.
The beach at Normandy, France was the site of the D-Day invasion on June 6, 1944, one of the most consequential military operations in history.
Camps Bay Beach in Cape Town, South Africa sits beneath the dramatic Twelve Apostles mountain range and is one of Africa's most scenic beaches.
Key West, the southernmost point in the continental United States, is only about 90 miles from Cuba and has beaches with warm water year-round.
The Beach Boys' 'Surfin' USA' was released in 1963 and became one of the defining songs of American beach culture.
Tide pooling is best done during low spring tides, which occur during full and new moons when gravitational pull is strongest.
The Cayman Islands have no rivers or streams, so their beaches have exceptionally clear water with visibility often exceeding 100 feet.
Stingrays bury themselves in the sand in shallow beach waters, which is why beach safety experts recommend doing the 'stingray shuffle' when wading.
Fire Island, a barrier island off Long Island, New York, has no roads and is accessible only by ferry, with 26 miles of car-free beaches.
The annual Eddie Aikau Big Wave Invitational at Waimea Bay, Hawaii only runs when waves reach a minimum of 20 feet.
National Sunscreen Day encourages proper sun protection at the beach and beyond.
Clifton Beach in Cape Town is a series of four sheltered coves separated by granite boulders, each with its own distinct atmosphere.
The world's oldest known sandcastle-building competition has been held in Cannon Beach, Oregon since 1964.
Conch shells, often associated with tropical beaches, were used as trumpets by indigenous peoples throughout the Caribbean for thousands of years.
About 70% of the Earth's surface is covered by water, but only a tiny fraction of the coastline has sandy beaches.
Coronado Beach in San Diego features the iconic Hotel del Coronado, which has welcomed guests since 1888 and inspired the setting for the movie 'Some Like It Hot.'
The Great Ocean Road in Victoria, Australia stretches 150 miles and passes spectacular beaches, the Twelve Apostles sea stacks, and rainforest.
Wrightsville Beach, North Carolina was one of the birthplaces of East Coast surfing culture in the 1960s.
A beach cusp is a crescent-shaped sand formation that occurs naturally at the shoreline, typically spaced at regular intervals.
Vieques Island in Puerto Rico has Mosquito Bay, one of the brightest bioluminescent bays in the world, near its beautiful beaches.
The first sunglasses specifically designed for sun protection were sold on the Atlantic City boardwalk in 1929 by Sam Foster.
National Safe Boating Week begins annually in late May, promoting safety in coastal waters near beaches.
Langkawi in Malaysia is a duty-free island with beaches like Pantai Cenang that combine white sand with lush tropical jungle backdrop.
The concept of the boardwalk was invented to keep sand out of the fancy hotels in Atlantic City, New Jersey.
Ghost crabs, found on sandy beaches worldwide, can run up to 10 miles per hour and have 360-degree vision.
Cape Hatteras National Seashore in North Carolina was the first national seashore established in the United States in 1953.
The annual Hermosa Beach Ironman competition in California combines one mile of surfing, one mile of running on sand, and one mile of paddleboarding.
Negril Beach in Jamaica stretches seven miles and is famous for its cliff jumping at Rick's Cafe and vibrant sunset parties.
Sea oats are protected by law in many US coastal states because they help stabilize sand dunes against hurricanes and erosion.
Mediterranean monk seals are one of the most endangered marine mammals and are occasionally spotted on remote beaches in Greece and Turkey.
World Turtle Day raises awareness about protecting turtles and their beach nesting habitats.
The Hamptons' Coopers Beach in Southampton was named America's best beach in 2010 by coastal expert Dr. Stephen Leatherman.
Praia da Marinha in the Algarve, Portugal is known for its dramatic limestone cliffs, natural arches, and clear turquoise water.
Beach morning glory is a common tropical beach plant that helps prevent erosion with its extensive root system and vine-like growth.
Zanzibar was once the world's largest clove producer, and its Stone Town beaches still carry the aroma of spices from nearby markets.
The Maldives consists of 1,192 coral islands grouped into 26 atolls, with beaches that sit barely above sea level.
Folly Beach, South Carolina is nicknamed 'The Edge of America' and is known for its consistent surf breaks and laid-back atmosphere.
The sand on Normandy's beaches still contains tiny metallic fragments from the D-Day invasion, over 80 years later.
Thailand has celebrated its annual beach cleanup day to maintain its famous coastlines and combat marine pollution.
The annual Volcom Pipe Pro surfing competition takes place at Pipeline on Oahu's North Shore during January and February.
Plankton blooms near beaches can cause red tides, which discolor the water and can be harmful to marine life and humans.
Fraser Island in Australia is the world's largest sand island, stretching over 76 miles with freshwater lakes perched on sand.
Ocean Isle Beach in North Carolina is a barrier island that was largely undeveloped until the 1950s and still retains a quiet, family-friendly character.
Barceloneta Beach in Barcelona was largely created artificially for the 1992 Olympic Games and has since become one of Europe's most popular urban beaches.
The Azores, a Portuguese archipelago in the mid-Atlantic, features beaches with black volcanic sand and natural hot springs near the shore.
A sandbar is a ridge of sand built up by waves and currents, sometimes creating shallow areas far from shore that are dangerous for swimmers.
Surfer's ear, or exostosis, is a condition where bone grows abnormally in the ear canal from repeated exposure to cold water and wind.
The first 4th of July fireworks over a beach were held at coastal towns along the East Coast in the early 1800s.
Manuel Antonio Beach in Costa Rica is located within a national park and is surrounded by tropical rainforest where monkeys swing in the trees above the sand.
The world record for the longest line of beach towels was set with over 16,000 towels laid end to end.
Rehoboth Beach, Delaware has been called 'The Nation's Summer Capital' due to its proximity to Washington, D.C.
Sand dollars are actually a type of sea urchin, and the white discs found on beaches are their dried skeletons.
Maui's Kaanapali Beach was one of the first planned resort areas in Hawaii and features a cliff diving ceremony at Black Rock every evening.
The coast of Maine has more than 3,400 miles of tidal coastline, featuring rocky beaches, tide pools, and dramatic cliff formations.
National Marina Day celebrates the recreational boating community and coastal marina access.
Beach umbrellas were first used in ancient Egypt and Rome to shield royalty from the sun, centuries before they became a beach staple.
Turquoise water at tropical beaches is caused by sunlight reflecting off the white sandy bottom through shallow, clear water.
The island of Curaçao in the Caribbean has over 35 beaches, many hidden in secluded coves along its rugged coastline.
Leatherback sea turtles, the largest turtles in the world, can weigh over 2,000 pounds and nest on beaches from Costa Rica to Florida.
The Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk in California, opened in 1907, is the oldest surviving amusement park on the West Coast.
Anguilla in the Caribbean has 33 beaches on an island that is only 16 miles long and 3 miles wide.
The summer solstice marks the beginning of peak beach season in the Northern Hemisphere.
Mussel shells found on beaches attach to rocks using byssal threads that are five times stronger than human tendons.
Palm Beach, Florida became a fashionable resort destination in the 1890s after Henry Flagler built luxury hotels and extended his railroad there.
The Whitsunday Islands in Australia have 74 islands, most of them uninhabited, with some of the most pristine beaches in the Great Barrier Reef region.
A tombolo is a sand or gravel bar that connects an island to the mainland, creating a unique beach formation.
Pelicans are iconic beach birds that dive from heights of up to 70 feet to catch fish, hitting the water at speeds of nearly 40 mph.
The average depth of sand on most beaches is only about 1 to 3 meters, beneath which lies rock or clay.
Barcelona's city beaches were contaminated and neglected until major cleanup and renovation ahead of the 1992 Summer Olympics.
Galveston Island, Texas has 32 miles of Gulf Coast beaches and was once the largest and wealthiest city in Texas before the 1900 hurricane.
Some beaches in Japan have musical roads nearby designed so that driving over them at certain speeds produces a melody.
The Chesapeake Bay Bridge-Tunnel connects Virginia Beach to the Eastern Shore and is one of the longest bridge-tunnel complexes in the world.
Moonstone Beach in Cambria, California is named for the translucent moonstone agates that can be found among its pebbles.
The Boxing Day Tsunami in 2004 devastated beaches across the Indian Ocean, affecting 14 countries and killing over 230,000 people.
Driftwood found on beaches may have traveled thousands of miles on ocean currents before washing ashore.
The beach at Dunkirk, France was the site of a massive evacuation of Allied troops in 1940, with over 338,000 soldiers rescued.
Okinawa, Japan has some of the best beaches in Asia, with star-shaped sand grains that are actually the exoskeletons of tiny marine organisms.
Venice Beach's original canals, built in 1905 by developer Abbot Kinney to replicate Venice, Italy, still exist a few blocks from the beach.
South Padre Island, Texas is a popular spring break destination and also one of the most important sea turtle nesting sites in the US.
The Great Australian Bight features dramatic cliffs that drop hundreds of feet to beaches below, and is a calving ground for southern right whales.
Beach clubs originated on the French Riviera in the 1920s and have since become popular at luxury resorts worldwide.
Shipwreck Beach (Navagio) in Zakynthos, Greece was named after the MV Panagiotis, which ran aground there in 1980 while allegedly smuggling contraband.
Sea glass takes 20 to 40 years to be tumbled smooth by ocean waves, and the rarest colors are red, orange, and turquoise.
National Lighthouse Day celebrates the maritime beacons that have guided ships away from dangerous beaches and shorelines.
Playa Flamenco in Culebra, Puerto Rico is consistently rated as one of the top beaches in the world and features a rusted WWII tank on its sand.
Beach renourishment projects pump sand from offshore deposits to widen eroding beaches, typically lasting 5 to 10 years before needing to be repeated.
The Hamptons beach houses can cost tens of millions of dollars, with oceanfront properties among the most expensive real estate in the United States.
Hamelin Pool in Shark Bay, Australia has beaches with stromatolites, living fossils that are among the oldest life forms on Earth at 3.5 billion years old.
The annual Lifeguard Championships bring together beach lifeguards from around the world to compete in ocean rescue events.
The Portuguese island of Madeira has beaches made of dark volcanic pebbles and boulders rather than traditional sand.
Wrightsville Beach, North Carolina hosted the first East Coast surfing championship in 1966.
Cape Cod's Nauset Beach has been retreating inland due to erosion at a rate of about 3 feet per year.
The Canary Islands, off the northwest coast of Africa, have beaches ranging from golden sand to black volcanic rock, with year-round warm weather.
Seagulls can drink both fresh and salt water thanks to special glands above their eyes that filter out the salt.
La Jolla Cove in San Diego is a small beach known for its incredibly clear water and large colony of harbor seals.
The annual Burning Man festival takes place on the playa (dry lakebed) of Nevada's Black Rock Desert, a type of ancient beach.
Amelia Island in Florida has 13 miles of beaches and is the only place in the US that has been under eight different flags.
The tradition of beach bonfires dates back centuries, with coastal communities using fires for warmth, cooking, and signaling ships.
National Oceans Month is observed throughout June to highlight the importance of ocean and coastal conservation.
One cubic foot of beach sand contains approximately 1 million individual grains.
Big Sur's Pfeiffer Beach in California features a natural keyhole rock arch through which sunset light creates a spectacular purple glow in winter.
The traditional Hawaiian outrigger canoe was one of the first watercraft used for surfing, predating modern surfboards by centuries.
Noosa Main Beach in Queensland, Australia is one of the few north-facing beaches on the Australian east coast, sheltered from southern swells.
The Galápagos Islands have beaches where marine iguanas, sea lions, and blue-footed boobies share the sand with visitors.
The world's first artificially created beach was Monaco's Larvotto Beach, where sand was imported and placed over a rocky shoreline.
Sanderlings, small shorebirds that chase waves back and forth on the sand, migrate up to 6,000 miles between their Arctic breeding grounds and winter beaches.
Long Beach, California was once known as 'The Coney Island of the Pacific' and now hosts the Queen Mary ocean liner as a floating hotel.
Mexico's Hidden Beach (Playa del Amor) on the Marieta Islands is located inside a crater-like opening, accessible only by swimming through a tunnel.
International Lifeguard Appreciation Day recognizes the vital role of beach lifeguards in keeping swimmers safe.
The annual sand migration along beaches, known as longshore drift, can move millions of tons of sand along a coastline each year.
Tybee Island, Georgia is known as 'Savannah's Beach' and features a historic lighthouse that has guided ships since 1736.
The world's busiest shipping lanes pass near popular beaches in places like the English Channel, Strait of Malacca, and New York Harbor.
Olive ridley sea turtles arrive by the hundreds of thousands on certain beaches in synchronized mass nesting events called arribadas.
Destin, Florida is known as 'The World's Luckiest Fishing Village' and has beaches with emerald-green Gulf waters.
The wave height record for surfing was set by Sebastian Steudtner riding an 86-foot wave at Nazaré, Portugal.
Block Island, Rhode Island has 17 miles of beaches and was named one of the 'last great places in the Western Hemisphere' by The Nature Conservancy.
Beach sand composition varies dramatically: Hawaii has olivine, Bermuda has coral, and the Saharan coast has quartz from the desert.
The annual International Kite Festival at the beach draws kite enthusiasts from around the world with elaborate, artistic kite designs.
The first recorded shark attack in American waters occurred near a beach. The most famous series of attacks happened along the New Jersey shore in 1916.
Sargassum seaweed, which washes up in massive quantities on Caribbean and Gulf beaches, originates from the Sargasso Sea in the North Atlantic.
The Amalfi Coast's Positano Beach was a poor fishing village until it became a fashionable resort destination in the 1950s.
Assateague Island's wild horses are descendants of domesticated horses brought to the island in the 17th century.
Laguna Beach, California has over 20 named beaches and coves along 7 miles of coastline, each with its own character.
The phenomenon of 'sneaker waves,' sudden large waves on otherwise calm beaches, is particularly dangerous on the Pacific Northwest coast.
National Boating Safety Week promotes safe practices in coastal waters and near beaches.
The world's smallest beach is Gulpiyuri Beach in Spain, which sits 100 meters inland with seawater flowing through underground caves.
St. Pete Beach, Florida stretches along a barrier island and has been named the best beach in America by TripAdvisor.
Waimea Bay on Oahu's North Shore transforms from a placid swimming beach in summer to hosting 40-foot waves in winter.
The coastal town of Byron Bay in New South Wales, Australia is the easternmost point of the Australian mainland.
Kiawah Island, South Carolina has 10 miles of pristine beach and is recognized as one of the top resort islands in the United States.
Blue-ringed octopuses, found in tide pools on Australian beaches, are one of the most venomous marine animals despite being the size of a golf ball.
Half Moon Bay, California hosts the annual Mavericks big wave surfing competition, where waves can reach 60 feet.
The original Muscle Beach in Santa Monica, California launched the fitness revolution in the 1930s and 40s, attracting bodybuilders and acrobats.
Nantucket Island, Massachusetts has beaches named for whaling history and maintains a charming seaside character with strict architectural guidelines.
International Whale Day promotes the protection of whales often seen from beaches during migration season.
The Pacific Ocean's 'Ring of Fire' creates volcanic beaches in places like New Zealand, Japan, Indonesia, and the Philippines.
A beach's grade refers to its slope angle, with steeper beaches typically composed of larger pebbles and gentler beaches made of fine sand.
Miami Beach is actually a barrier island separated from mainland Miami by Biscayne Bay, connected by a series of causeways.
Montauk Point at the eastern tip of Long Island marks the end of the Hamptons beaches and features a lighthouse commissioned by George Washington in 1792.
The annual Henley-on-Todd Regatta in Australia is a beach race where participants run carrying bottomless boats across a dry riverbed.
Bondi to Coogee Coastal Walk in Sydney passes six beaches and features dramatic cliff-top views along 3.7 miles of trail.
First International Beach Clean-up Day was organized in 1986 by the Ocean Conservancy.
The phenomenon of 'glacial flour,' extremely fine rock particles from glaciers, creates the distinctive turquoise color of beaches in places like Lake Louise and Iceland.
The island nation of Tuvalu in the Pacific has some of the most vulnerable beaches in the world due to sea level rise, with an average elevation of just 6.6 feet.
Dune buggies became popular on California beaches in the 1950s and 60s, evolving from modified Volkswagen Beetles.
Newport Beach, California is home to The Wedge, a bodyboarding and bodysurfing spot known for waves that can exceed 20 feet due to a unique jetty formation.
The term 'tsunami' comes from the Japanese words for harbor ('tsu') and wave ('nami'), referencing the devastating waves that can impact beaches.
Cumberland Island, Georgia is an undeveloped barrier island with 18 miles of beaches, wild horses, and the ruins of Carnegie family mansions.
Horseshoe crabs have blue blood that is used in medical testing and is worth up to $60,000 per gallon.
The first beach lifeguard stand in the United States was set up at South Beach, Miami in the 1920s.
World Wetlands Day recognizes the importance of coastal wetlands adjacent to beaches.
Pebble beaches, like those along the coast of Nice, France, are formed in areas where strong wave action washes away finer sand particles.
The concept of 'blue spaces,' including beaches, has been scientifically linked to improved mental health and reduced stress levels.
Outer Banks' Cape Hatteras is known as the 'Graveyard of the Atlantic' due to the more than 1,000 shipwrecks along its shores.
Plastic pollution affects every beach on Earth, with an estimated 8 million metric tons of plastic entering the oceans annually.
The annual Quicksilver Pro France surfing competition is held at Hossegor, a beach town on France's Atlantic coast known for powerful barrel waves.
Huntington Beach, California holds the trademark for the nickname 'Surf City, USA' and hosts the US Open of Surfing every summer.
Kangaroo Island off South Australia has Vivonne Bay, named Australia's best beach, with pristine white sand and crystal-clear water.
Martha's Vineyard hosts the annual Polar Bear Swim on New Year's Day at State Beach in Edgartown.
Clam digging at low tide is a traditional beach activity along the New England and Pacific Northwest coasts.
The world's first underwater post office operates off a beach in Vanuatu, where divers can mail waterproof postcards.
Diani Beach in Kenya stretches for over 10 miles along the Indian Ocean and is backed by coral reefs and coastal forests home to colobus monkeys.
The annual Polar Plunge events in February raise money for Special Olympics as participants run into freezing ocean water at beaches.
Rockaway Beach in Queens, New York is the largest urban beach in the United States, stretching over 5 miles.
The phenomenon of 'beach whistling,' where sand produces sounds when walked on, occurs at beaches with rounded grains of uniform size.
Microplastics smaller than 5 millimeters have been found on beaches in the most remote locations, including uninhabited Arctic islands.
Captiva Island, Florida is connected to Sanibel Island by a bridge and is named for the legend that pirate José Gaspar held captive women there.
Easter Island (Rapa Nui) in the Pacific has Anakena Beach, where ancient Polynesians first landed and massive moai statues stand near the shore.
The annual Sandcastle Classic in Galveston, Texas is one of the largest sandcastle competitions in the United States.
Lake Michigan has some of the best freshwater beaches in the world, with sandy shores that rival ocean beaches in beauty.
The annual swim between Alcatraz Island and the San Francisco shore covers about 1.5 miles of cold, treacherous water near Ocean Beach.
Magnetic Island in Queensland, Australia was named by Captain James Cook in 1770 when his ship's compass was affected by the island's magnetic rocks.
Cockleshell Beach in St. Kitts overlooks the neighboring island of Nevis and is one of the most scenic beach settings in the Caribbean.
Beach wheelchairs, designed with large balloon tires to roll across sand, have made beaches more accessible to people with mobility challenges.
The Cornish coast in England has over 300 beaches and is known for its world-class surfing at spots like Fistral Beach in Newquay.
Pismo Beach, California was once famous for its enormous clam population and now hosts the annual Clam Festival dating back to 1946.
International Day for Biological Diversity highlights the unique ecosystems found in beach and coastal habitats.
A beach's backshore is the area above the normal high tide line that is only submerged during storms and extreme high tides.
The annual Vans US Open of Surfing at Huntington Beach draws over 500,000 spectators each summer.
Cocoa Beach, Florida hosted the filming of 'I Dream of Jeannie' and the TV show helped popularize the area as a beach destination in the 1960s.
The annual flying of sea turtle hatchlings, where baby turtles instinctively race from their beach nests to the ocean, is one of nature's most dramatic beach events.
Varadero Beach in Cuba stretches for 12 miles along the Hicacos Peninsula and is considered one of the best beaches in the Caribbean.
The annual surfing dog competition at Del Mar Beach in San Diego features dogs of all sizes riding waves on specially designed surfboards.
Beach erosion hotlines exist in several US states, allowing coastal residents to report significant shoreline changes after storms.
The longest pier in the world extending from a beach is the Progreso Pier in Mexico, stretching over 4 miles into the Gulf of Mexico.
Jericoacoara Beach in northeastern Brazil was named one of the 10 most beautiful beaches in the world, featuring massive sand dunes and freshwater lagoons.
The famous Beach Road in Chennai, India (Marina Beach) runs along the second-longest urban beach in the world at 8 miles.
National Maritime Day honors the maritime contributions and the importance of coastal economies including beach tourism.
The Great Barrier Reef Marine Park off the coast of Queensland includes over 900 islands, many with pristine sandy beaches.
El Nido in Palawan, Philippines is known for its towering limestone karst cliffs and hidden lagoon beaches accessible only by kayak.
The Normandy beaches used in the D-Day invasion — Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword — were code names, not the original French names.
Hapuna Beach on Hawaii's Big Island has been ranked the best white sand beach in the United States by multiple travel publications.
The annual polar bear swim at English Bay in Vancouver, Canada has been a New Year's Day tradition since 1920.
Drift seeds, also called sea beans, are tropical plant seeds that wash up on beaches thousands of miles from where they fell into the water.
Clearwater Marine Aquarium in Florida, near Clearwater Beach, is home to Winter the dolphin made famous by the movie 'Dolphin Tale.'
The annual Sandcastle Competition at Imperial Beach, California is the largest sand sculpting event in the United States.
Matira Beach in Bora Bora is one of the few public beaches on the island and is consistently rated among the best beaches in the South Pacific.
Beach roses (Rosa rugosa) are hardy shrubs that thrive in sandy coastal soil and produce edible rose hips rich in vitamin C.
The famous Twelve Apostles rock formations along Australia's Great Ocean Road were originally called 'Sow and Piglets' before being renamed for tourism.
World Reef Awareness Day highlights the importance of coral reefs to beach ecosystems.
Punta Cana in the Dominican Republic has 20 miles of white sand beaches lined with coconut palms and is the most visited destination in the Caribbean.
Razor clams burrow into the sand at speeds of up to 9 inches per second when disturbed, making them one of the fastest diggers on the beach.
Bathsheba Beach in Barbados features mushroom-shaped rock formations carved by Atlantic waves and is a popular surf spot on the island's east coast.
The concept of the 'beach read,' a light novel meant for vacation reading, became a publishing category in the 1980s.
Praia de Benagil in Portugal features a sea cave with a skylight opening in the ceiling, creating one of the most photographed beach caves in the world.
National Lighthouse and Lighthouse Preservation Day marks the anniversary of the Lighthouse Act signed by George Washington in 1789.
Clifton Beaches in Cape Town, South Africa are among the most expensive real estate areas in Africa, with beachfront properties worth millions.
The sand on some beaches in the Azores is a deep purple-black color due to the volcanic basalt rock of the islands.
Koh Lipe in Thailand is nicknamed 'The Maldives of Thailand' for its crystal-clear water and white sand beaches.
Beach stone stacking, the art of balancing rocks into towers, has become a popular meditative activity but is controversial because it can disturb wildlife habitat.
Huntington State Beach in California was designated as the first surfing reserve in the United States in 2018.
The annual Semana Santa (Holy Week) beach pilgrimage in the Philippines draws millions of people to coastal towns.
The Emerald Coast (Costa Smeralda) of Sardinia, Italy was developed as a luxury resort destination by the Aga Khan in the 1960s.
Portuguese man-of-war tentacles can still sting even after being washed ashore and dried on the beach for days.
Kuta Beach in Bali was the first beach in Indonesia to become an international tourist destination, drawing surfers since the 1930s.
The phenomenon of 'sea foam' on beaches is caused by dissolved organic matter in the ocean being churned up by waves and wind.
Diamond Beach in Iceland gets its name from chunks of glacial ice that wash ashore and glitter like diamonds on the black volcanic sand.
Copacabana's famous black and white wave-patterned sidewalk was designed by Brazilian landscape architect Roberto Burle Marx.
National Water Safety Month raises awareness about drowning prevention at beaches, pools, and open water.
The annual sea turtle release events at South Padre Island, Texas draw hundreds of spectators who watch rehabilitated turtles return to the Gulf of Mexico.
Beach towels became common in the 1950s and were originally much smaller than today's oversized versions that double as lounging mats.
Ipanema and Copacabana beaches in Rio are separated by the rocky headland of Arpoador, which is famous for its sunset viewing point.
The Maldives has 187 resort islands, each occupying its own small island with beaches that circle the entire perimeter.
Sandy Hook, a barrier peninsula in New Jersey, has some of the northernmost swimming beaches in the New York metropolitan area and is part of Gateway National Recreation Area.
